title: Find Kth Bit in Nth Binary String
content:
Given two positive integers n and k, the binary string Sn is formed as follows:

• S1 = "0"

• Si = Si - 1 + "1" + reverse(invert(Si - 1)) for i > 1

Where + denotes the concatenation operation, reverse(x) returns the reversed string x, and invert(x) inverts all the bits in x (0 changes to 1 and 1 changes to 0).

For example, the first four strings in the above sequence are:

• S1 = "0"

• S2 = "011"

• S3 = "0111001"

• S4 = "011100110110001"

Return the kth bit in Sn. It is guaranteed that k is valid for the given n.

Example 1:

Input: n = 3, k = 1
Output: "0"
Explanation: S3 is "0111001".
    The 1st bit is "0".

Example 2:

Input: n = 4, k = 11
Output: "1"
Explanation: S4 is "011100110110001".
    The 11th bit is "1".

Constraints:

• 1 <= n <= 20

• 1 <= k <= 2n - 1

hints: Since n is small, we can simply simulate the process of constructing S1 to Sn.

solutions.explanation: Recursively find the kth bit by understanding the structure of the string. S_n = S_(n-1) + '1' + invert(reverse(S_(n-1))). For position k, determine which part it falls into and recurse accordingly.
solutions.complexity: Time: O(n) - recursion depth is n. Space: O(n) - recursion stack.
